methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

path: Proxy appends caller address, forwards to PoolManager.dispatch
data_flows.trust_changes: User input amount validated; proxy appends authenticated sender; module trusts sender from calldata tail
exposure_risks: Supply cap check uses potentially stale supplyIndex if called before updateInterestIndexs
manipulation_risks: Fee-on-transfer tokens handled via balance checks but could cause revert instead of silent loss

path: ValidateLogic.validateCrossBorrowERC20Account checks health factor and per-group collateral
transformations: Price oracle converts amounts to baseCurrency for LTV check
data_flows.trust_changes: Oracle price data trusted for collateral valuation; external IRM trusted for rate calculation
exposure_risks: Oracle manipulation could inflate collateral value allowing excessive borrowing
manipulation_risks: Rounding in _getUserERC20DebtInBaseCurrency can round tiny debts to 0, allowing dust debt accumulation that evades health factor checks

path: Yield cap checked against inflated total supply
transformations: borrowAmount -> yieldAmount via external protocol deposit
data_flows.trust_changes: Pool yield module trusted for borrowing; external yield protocol (Lido/Etherfi/MakerDAO) trusted for deposit/withdraw
exposure_risks: External protocol could fail or return less than expected
Rebasing token balance changes between operations
manipulation_risks: Using borrowIndex instead of supplyIndex inflates calculated total supply, allowing borrowing beyond intended yield cap

path: Bidder calls isolateAuction with bid amounts
ValidateLogic checks loan status and health factor
After auction duration, liquidation can be called
transformations: bidAmount vs debt for surplus/deficit calculation
data_flows.trust_changes: Oracle price trusted for liquidation trigger; auction timing from block.timestamp
validation_points: Health factor below threshold triggers auction
exposure_risks: Block timestamp manipulation could affect auction timing
Oracle delay could trigger unfair liquidations
manipulation_risks: Auction duration edge cases at boundary timestamps

path: _accrueFeeToTreasury: (currBorrow - prevBorrow) * feeFactor -> scaled by supplyIndex -> accruedFee
updateInterestRates: recalculate utilization, group rates, asset rate
transformations: supplyRate -> linearInterest multiplier over time delta
borrowRate -> compoundedInterest multiplier over time delta (binomial approx)
data_flows.trust_changes: External IRM trusted for rate calculation; block.timestamp trusted for time delta
validation_points: toUint128() safe cast reverts on overflow
exposure_risks: uint128 overflow for indices after extreme long-term operation (unlikely but theoretically possible)
Compounded interest uses binomial approximation that slightly undercharges
manipulation_risks: Manipulating block.timestamp (minor, ±15s) could slightly affect interest calculations

path: Proxy appends msg.sender (20 bytes) to calldata
dispatch strips 4-byte selector, appends proxy address (20 bytes)
delegatecall to moduleImpl with augmented calldata
Module extracts msgSender from last 40 bytes, proxyAddr from last 20 bytes
transformations: Original calldata -> prepend dispatch selector -> append caller -> append proxy addr
calldatasize used to locate trailing params
data_flows.trust_changes: Trust established via trustedSenders mapping; only proxies created by PoolManager are trusted
exposure_risks: If msgDataLength check is insufficient, truncated calldata could lead to incorrect msgSender extraction
manipulation_risks: Direct calls to dispatch from non-proxy addresses correctly rejected

path: InterestLogic.updateInterestIndexs (accrues new fee in same call)
transformations: Scaled accruedFee * current supplyIndex = actual fee amount
Fee amount -> scaled supply tokens for treasury
data_flows.trust_changes: Only pool admin can trigger collection; treasury address from addressProvider
exposure_risks: Double-scaling: fee scaled by supplyIndex at accrual, then unscaled by current supplyIndex — correct but any index regression would cause issues
manipulation_risks: Fee collection mints supply tokens without actual token transfer — no liquidity impact but inflates supply accounting

core_rules: Health factor must be >= 1e18 for cross-margin positions
Borrowing is per-group: collateral in classGroup X backs debt in borrowGroup X
Isolate-mode NFTs can only have one loan per token
Yield borrowing is capped at a percentage of total supply per asset and per staker
Interest accrues linearly for supply and compounded (binomial approximation) for borrow
Liquidation requires health factor below threshold with bonus incentive
Auction-based liquidation for isolated NFT positions with time-bounded bidding
economic_incentives: Suppliers earn interest from borrower payments minus protocol fee
Borrowers pay compounded interest on debt
Liquidators receive liquidation bonus for clearing bad debt
Yield stakers earn yield spread (external yield - borrow cost)
Auction bidders compete for liquidated NFTs
Protocol collects fee factor percentage of interest spread
governance_mechanisms: Pool admin can create/configure pools, assets, groups, and parameters
Emergency admin can pause/unpause pools and global protocol
Oracle admin configures price feed sources
ACL manager uses role-based access control via OpenZeppelin AccessControl
Bot admin can force-unstake yield positions below health threshold

edge_cases.scenario: YieldLogic.executeYieldBorrowERC20 uses groupData.borrowIndex instead of assetData.supplyIndex to calculate total supply for yield cap check
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Total supply should be calculated as totalScaledCrossSupply.rayMul(assetData.supplyIndex) to get actual deposits
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Total supply is calculated as totalScaledCrossSupply.rayMul(groupData.borrowIndex) where groupData is the yield group, inflating the cap
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Since borrowIndex grows faster than supplyIndex (due to protocol fees and rate spread), the yield cap check becomes progressively more permissive over time. Yield managers can borrow more than the intended percentage of actual deposits, potentially draining available liquidity beyond safe levels.

edge_cases.scenario: Tiny debts round to zero in _getUserERC20DebtInBaseCurrency
edge_cases.expected_behavior: All debt amounts should be accurately reflected in health factor calculation
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Division (assetPrice * userTotalDebt) / (10 ** underlyingDecimals) can truncate to 0 for dust amounts when assetPrice is small relative to decimals
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: An attacker could open many tiny borrow positions across groups/assets that individually round to 0 debt in base currency. Collectively these could accumulate meaningful untracked debt. Health factor would show infinity while actual debt exists.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: totalUnstakeFine should decrease when a fine is paid during repayment
edge_cases.actual_behavior: totalUnstakeFine only increases in _unstake but is never decremented. The fine amount stays in the contract but the global counter monotonically increases.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: No direct exploit, but the totalUnstakeFine variable becomes an inaccurate tracker of outstanding fines over time. Could affect off-chain monitoring or any future logic depending on this value.

edge_cases.scenario: First yield staker share manipulation via interest accrual timing
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Debt shares should fairly represent proportional debt obligations
edge_cases.actual_behavior: ShareUtils uses +1 virtual offset (totalShares+1, totalAssets+1). If first staker stakes 1 wei, then significant interest accrues before second staker, second staker gets disproportionately fewer shares per unit of debt.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Limited by the +1 virtual share offset and the fact that interest accrual is bounded. The attacker cannot directly inflate totalDebt (only interest accrual increases it). However, if the first stake is very small and time passes, the share price diverges.

edge_cases.scenario: Bot admin can trigger repay pulling funds from NFT owner
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Only NFT owner should authorize token transfers from their wallet
edge_cases.actual_behavior: In _repay, if botAdmin calls and extraAmount > 0, safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, ...) attempts to pull from the NFT owner who may have pre-existing approvals
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If NFT owner has approved the YieldStaking contract for the underlying asset, a malicious or compromised botAdmin could time the repay to maximize extraAmount pulled from the owner (e.g., during low claimed yield). The owner cannot prevent this without revoking approval.

edge_cases.scenario: Rebasing token balance divergence between unstake and claim in Lido/Etherfi
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Withdrawal amount should match the value at time of withdrawal request
edge_cases.actual_behavior: sd.withdrawAmount is set at unstake time. For stETH/eETH, the actual balance may have changed due to rebasing between unstake and claim. The protocolClaimWithdraw returns actual ETH received which may differ.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: The _repay function handles the difference by computing extraAmount or remainAmount. However, if a negative rebase occurs (slashing), claimed yield could be less than expected, forcing the NFT owner to cover a larger shortfall.

edge_cases.scenario: Health factor check in _stake uses unstakeHeathFactor as minimum
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Staking should require a comfortable buffer above liquidation threshold
edge_cases.actual_behavior: require(hf >= nc.unstakeHeathFactor) — if unstakeHeathFactor is set close to 1.0, users can stake right at the boundary with no buffer for price movements
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Misconfigured unstakeHeathFactor could allow positions that are immediately at risk of forced unstaking, causing cascading liquidations during volatility. Admin configuration error could enable this.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: availableLiquidity should reflect actual token balance during flash loan
edge_cases.actual_behavior: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan/erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan bypass availableLiquidity accounting. During flash loan callback, availableLiquidity still shows pre-loan amount.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Protected by nonReentrant guard preventing reentrant borrows during flash loan. If reentrancy guard were bypassed, the stale availableLiquidity could allow double-spending.

edge_cases.scenario: erc20IncreaseCrossSupply in executeCollectFeeToTreasury mints supply without token transfer
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Supply increase should correspond to actual token deposits
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Treasury supply is increased as accounting entry only — backed by interest already paid by borrowers. No physical token transfer occurs.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If fee accrual calculation is incorrect, treasury could claim more than actual interest collected, but normal flow is correct. The available liquidity is not increased, maintaining solvency.

edge_cases.scenario: Compounded interest binomial approximation undercharges for very long periods
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Interest should accurately reflect time-weighted compounding
edge_cases.actual_behavior: 3-term binomial approximation (1 + nx + n(n-1)/2 * x^2 + n(n-1)(n-2)/6 * x^3) slightly underestimates true compound interest. Error grows with time gap and rate.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attackers could avoid interacting with the protocol for long periods to benefit from the undercharge. The error is typically <0.01% for reasonable rates and weekly updates, but could be significant with extreme rates and long gaps.

assumption_violations: Assumes all ERC20 tokens transfer exactly the specified amount (validated by balance checks, but reverts rather than adapting for fee-on-transfer tokens)
Assumes oracle prices are current and not manipulated — no TWAP or staleness checks beyond basic Chainlink round validation
Assumes supplyIndex and borrowIndex never overflow uint128 (~3.4e38, starting from 1e27 allows ~3.4e11x growth)
Assumes external yield protocols (Lido, Etherfi, MakerDAO) behave correctly and don't have exploits
Assumes WETH contract follows standard WETH9 behavior where transferFrom with src==msg.sender skips allowance check
Assumes block.timestamp is reasonably accurate (protocol uses it for interest calculations)
game_theory_risks: Yield stakers can borrow up to leverageFactor of NFT value — if the external yield protocol suffers losses, the protocol's lending pool bears the risk through increased debt share values
Auction bidders can wait until the last moment of auctionDuration to bid, potentially using MEV to outbid competitors
Liquidators choose which collateral to seize — they will always choose the most liquid/valuable collateral, leaving less desirable collateral in the protocol
Bot admin has significant power over yield positions — can force unstake and set fines up to maxUnstakeFine
Users can split borrows across many groups with dust amounts to evade accurate health factor computation due to rounding
integration_risks: Lido stETH is a rebasing token — balanceOf changes every block, requiring careful accounting in yield share calculations
EtherFi eETH is also rebasing — same concerns as stETH
sDAI (Savings DAI) uses ERC4626 vault shares — non-rebasing but exchange rate changes
Chainlink oracle staleness: only checks answeredInRound >= roundId and updatedAt != 0, no maximum age check
BendNFTOracle price could lag market conditions, enabling arbitrage against the protocol
YieldAccount.execute allows arbitrary calls to external contracts — if yield manager logic has flaws, could be exploited
WETH deposit/withdraw in yield staking creates ETH <-> WETH conversion points that could fail with insufficient gas
calculation_errors: CRITICAL: YieldLogic.executeYieldBorrowERC20 line 'vars.totalSupply = VaultLogic.erc20GetTotalCrossSupply(assetData, groupData.borrowIndex)' should use assetData.supplyIndex, not groupData.borrowIndex. This inflates the yield cap check denominator.
GenericLogic._getUserERC20DebtInBaseCurrency: (assetPrice * userTotalDebt) / (10 ** underlyingDecimals) — for tokens with many decimals and low prices, integer division truncates debt to 0
GenericLogic._getUserERC20BalanceInBaseCurrency: same truncation issue but for collateral (rounds down collateral which is conservative)
InterestLogic._accrueFeeToTreasury: applies .toUint128() on fee amount before adding to uint256 accruedFee — unnecessary restriction that could revert for very large fee amounts

token_semantics.notes: Used as wrappedNativeToken. VaultLogic.wrapNativeTokenInWallet relies on WETH9 transferFrom(self) behavior.
Lido staked ETH. Balance changes on every oracle report (rebase). Used in YieldEthStakingLido. Share calculation must account for balance changes between operations.
EtherFi eETH. Rebasing token similar to stETH. Used in YieldEthStakingEtherfi.
ERC4626 vault token. Non-rebasing shares model. Used in YieldSavingsDai. convertToAssets needed for proper valuation.
VaultLogic uses balance-before/after checks on all transfers, causing reverts for fee-on-transfer tokens rather than silent accounting errors. Decimal varies per asset.
NFT collateral. Price from BendNFTOracle floor price. Supply tracked by count, no decimal scaling.
